message: 220 human-human dialogs.

Intent is annotated using a hierarchical scheme that separates on-task intents (task-specific key actions) and off-task intents (common dialog acts). On-task intents are defined per dataset (e.g., elicitation, providing information, refusal in ANTI-SCAM; nine donation-related intents in PERSUASION FOR GOOD), while off-task intents include six general intents (open question, yes no question, positive answer, negative answer, responsive statement, nonresponsive statement) and six social intents (greeting, closing, apology, thanking, respond to thank, hold).
TransferTransfo and the hybrid model.

Automatic metrics: Perplexity (PPL); Response-Intent Prediction (RIP) and Response-Slot Prediction (RSP), where system response intents/slots are predicted by trained intent/slot classifiers and compared to human-annotated ground truth; and Extended Response-Intent Prediction (ERIP) & Extended Response-Slot Prediction (ERSP), where a predicted intent scores 1 if it matches ground truth otherwise scores p(I_predict|I_input) estimated from intent bigram frequencies. Human evaluation metrics: Fluency, Coherence, Engagement, Dialog Length, and Task Success Score (average types of personal information obtained), rated by volunteers and averaged.
It is competitive with other systems with access to larger data sources for some tasks.
They compare against Liu et al. (2015) and Yang et al. (2012).

They report accuracy and also use the standard TDT evaluation procedure with DET (Detection Error Trade-off) curves (trade-off between miss and false alarm probability); efficiency is measured as throughput per second.

Yes — they use supervised learning: they frame detection as a classification problem and train SVMs on labeled rumours and non-rumours, with pseudo-feedback features computed via a two-round training procedure.
Yes; they created their own dataset containing 202 confirmed rumours (from Sina Weibo's official rumour debunking service) and 202 non-rumours.
Chinese
They consider unconfirmed (novel) information with respect to trusted resources as an indication of rumours.
They compared with LDA, Doc-NADE, HTMM, and GMNTM.
Generative Document Evaluation and Document Classification.
CoNLL2003, OntoNotes 5.0, OntoNotes 4.0 (Chinese), MSRA, Weibo NER, and Resume NER.

They compute a relative positional encoding R_{t-j} = [... sin((t−j)/10000^{2i/d_k}) cos((t−j)/10000^{2i/d_k}) ...]^T which has sin terms that change sign for forward vs. backward offsets so it encodes direction and distance.  
They incorporate it into attention as A_rel_{t,j} = Q_t K_j^T + Q_t R_{t-j}^T + u^T K_j + v^T R_{t-j}, and Attn(Q,K,V)=softmax(A_rel)V.

Yes — TENER achieves superior performance than prevailing BiLSTM-based models and attains new state-of-the-art performance on two NER datasets when using GloVe 100d and CNN character embeddings, with further gains when using ELMo.
Unanswerable
OpenIE, SEMAFOR, SLING, the Standford KBP system, Memory Network, Variational Reasoning Network, ATHENA, and PowerAqua.
They evaluated on the MetaQA dataset.

The contributions of this paper are: (i) the adaptation of a state-of-the-art sequence to sequence (seq2seq) neural system to event detection and classification for Italian in a single step approach; (ii) an investigation on the quality of existing Italian word embeddings for this task; and (iii) a comparison against a state-of-the-art discrete classifier. The pre-trained models and scripts running the system (or re-train it) are publicly available.

The paper compares Bi-LSTM-CRF systems initialized with five Italian embeddings (Berardi2015 w2v; Berardi2015 glove; Fastext-It; ILC-ItWack; DH-FBK 100) against the FBK-HLT@EVENTI 2014 baseline (a cascade of two SVM classifiers).
Unanswerable

They first disambiguate each cluster word to WordNet synsets, choosing S* that minimizes total synset distance; generate candidate labels L as the union of all hypernyms of synsets in S*; and rank candidates by the sum of their WordNet pathwise distances to the synsets in S*, selecting the label with least total distance.

They train domain-specific Word2Vec (CBOW) embeddings for words and then apply k-means clustering to the embeddings of gender-associated words (running k-means 50 times and keeping the model with the lowest sum of squared errors).
Each consists of over 300K sentences.
The centroid of the cluster (as a strong baseline label) and a random baseline of 20% (for the Word Intrusion task).
They rank low overall and tend to produce flirtatious or retaliatory responses rather than polite refusals.
They collected 9960 HITs from 472 crowd workers.
Yes; the customised HeidelTime achieved an F1 value of 89.1 for partial identification and normalization.
Agglutinative and Fusional.
Yes — the model handles derivational boundaries (merged for Turkish) and inflectional groups and uses morphological outputs including lemmas and language-specific morphological tags.
Gold morphological features (oracle): the lemma followed by language-specific morphological tags (with additional information such as POS for some languages, e.g., Turkish).

They use the Semantic Scholar corpus (SemScholar), Springer Nature’s SciGraph, the Textbook Question Answering (TQA) corpus, the January 2018 English Wikipedia dataset, and the Flickr30K and COCO image-sentence matching benchmarks.
Unanswerable
Unanswerable
They compared with two supervised baselines: the direct combination baseline and the supervised pre-training baseline.
Partly: SciGraph and SemScholar are specific to the scientific domain, Textbook QA covers middle-school science, while Wikipedia, Flickr30K and COCO provide general-purpose image-text data.
They were extracted from Semantic Scholar PDFs using PDFFigures2.
Unanswerable
No; they explicitly state they did not experiment with additional sources of information such as lexicons.
The Weka baseline (Mohammad and Bravo-Marquez, 2017a).
Their intra-sentence attention RNN outperformed the Weka baseline on the development dataset but performed worse than the baseline on the test dataset.
The train, validation and test datasets provided for the shared task (Mohammad and Bravo-Marquez, 2017a), consisting of tweets annotated for four emotions: joy, sadness, fear, and anger.
They define hate speech as speech that promotes the dehumanization of people who are typically from minority groups and can incite hate crime.
English, French, and Arabic.
The explicitness of the tweet (directness), the type of hostility, the target attribute, the target group, and the annotator’s sentiment (how different people react).
The final dataset is composed of a pilot corpus of 100 tweets per language, and comparable corpora of 5,647 English tweets, 4,014 French tweets, and 3,353 Arabic tweets.

Semantic Web technologies can be used for semantic disambiguation (word-sense and syntactic) via topic-modeling, SPARQL queries and reasoning; for named-entity recognition and disambiguation by linking entities to knowledge bases and encoding entity types into training; and for handling non-standard speech and idioms using user-profile data and linked idiom datasets. They also enable translating and enriching knowledge bases (e.g., DBpedia) and creating bilingual embeddings or translated KGs to improve language models and MT quality, and support multilingual entity linking and knowledge-graph embeddings that have yielded measurable BLEU/METEOR/CHRF improvements.

Challenges include that applying SWT as pre- or post-editing is tedious for common words and often requires repeated application; reasoners have poor run-time performance; the use of multiple SW resources simultaneously has not been investigated; named entity recognition/linking remains imperfect; and KBs are predominantly in English, limiting cross-language coverage.
Unanswerable
Root mean square, zero crossing rate, moving window average, kurtosis and power spectral entropy.
Unanswerable
USC-TIMIT database.
No. Across the reported datasets in Table 3, BERT-Base (the teacher) has higher accuracies than LadaBERT.

Yes — they evaluate and find that a hybrid of matrix factorization (SVD) plus weight pruning outperforms pure weight pruning or pure SVD, producing smaller bias mean and deviation and a smoother weight distribution.
MNLI-m, MNLI-mm, SST-2, QQP, and QNLI.
News articles and corresponding highlights from the DailyMail website.
DailyMail

Yes; they include a neural abstractive baseline (NN-ABS) and compare to it. They report NN-WE consistently outperforms the purely abstractive model, and post-hoc tests show NN-SE (and ILP) are significantly better than LEAD, NN-WE, and NN-ABS.

They focus on hierarchical commonsense knowledge—ontologies of IS-A/hypernym (and other hierarchical) relations between single words and multi-word phrases—as well as non-hierarchical commonsense knowledge in the form of raw (unstructured) text.

They mean using the structure of the learned embedding space—so that hierarchical or partial-order relations are represented geometrically (e.g., in order embeddings a subcategory x is below a supercategory y by coordinate-wise inequalities x_i ≥ y_i)—to automatically enforce structural constraints and produce globally consistent structured predictions.
Yes — they used pretrained sentiment classifiers, including a multiplicative LSTM trained at the character level on a corpus of 82 million Amazon reviews (the OpenAI model).

Word unigrams and bigrams transformed with TF–IDF; distributed semantics via 300‑dimensional word2vec averaged per segment (with experiments using GloVe and Sent2Vec); and sentiment features from Empath lexical-category percentages plus pre-trained sentiment embeddings learned by a bidirectional LSTM trained on the Stanford Sentiment Treebank.
Unanswerable
Grouping, Connectivity and Discourse structure.
No; they were crowd-sourced non-expert annotators on Amazon Mechanical Turk.

The crowd-sourced AI2D annotations were created by non-expert annotators on Amazon Mechanical Turk and represent diagrams as Diagram Parse Graphs (DPGs) that model four element types (text, graphics, arrows, arrowheads) and conflate semantic relations and connections. The expert AI2D-RST annotations cover 1,000 AI2D diagrams and use a stand-off, multi-layer schema producing three graphs (Grouping, Connectivity, Discourse) that separate visual grouping, connections, and discourse structure, with high inter-annotator agreement measured by Fleiss’ κ (grouping κ=0.84, diagram types κ=0.78, connectivity κ=0.88, discourse structure κ=0.73).
Amazon Mechanical Turk
Unanswerable
They opt for the Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN) and a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).
They use the Clueweb09-derived dataset for baseline comparison and a new Wikipedia-derived dataset generated from Wikipedia crawl data seeded by the "List of Controversial articles."
cross-domain

They use Wikipedia’s “List of Controversial articles” (2018 and 2009) as a seed to crawl linked pages up to two hops for controversial weak-signal labels, and the Wikipedia random article endpoint to gather non-controversial (negative) seed pages.
Yes; they compare neural/semantic models to lexical baselines (TfIdf-SVM, language models LM-DBPedia and LM-wiki, and TILE-Clique), reporting results in Table 2.
About 64 M segments from YouTube videos comprising a total of 1.2 B tokens.
About 64M segments from YouTube videos comprising a total of 1.2B tokens.
1500-dimensional vectors extracted from the video frames at 1-second intervals, i.e., frame-level visual features similar to those used for large-scale image classification tasks.
Yes — the LSTM-based NMT models implement the scaled Luong attention mechanism.
An established LSTM-based NMT architecture using the (scaled) Luong attention mechanism, and the Transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017).

Symbolic rewriting is the application of equations as rewrite rules to transform terms—for example, given an equation s = t, terms of the form u[θ(s)] are rewritten to u[θ(t)] under a substitution θ (possibly trivial) within a context u.

They incorporate expert knowledge by specifying anchor variables X_i and linking them to latent topic factors Y_j via an additional mutual-information term I(X_i;Y_j) in the Anchored CorEx objective (specified as pairs R = {(i,j)}), which seeds topics with expert-specified words and preserves information about those anchors while CorEx discovers associated words; multiple anchors can attach to a factor and one anchor can attach to multiple factors.
They evaluate on the 20 Newsgroups and the i2b2 Obesity Challenge 2008 corpora.
No; they do not compare against LDA—experiments compare Anchored CorEx to a Naive Bayes baseline and the Related Work discusses SeededLDA.
Unanswerable
Peng and Dredze (2016) is the state-of-the-art NER system in Chinese Social media.
Sina Weibo.
They used a modified labelled Weibo NER corpus for NER in Chinese social media and the same unlabeled text from Sina Weibo (112,971,734 messages) as used by Peng and Dredze (2016).
XNLI (Cross-lingual NLI), MLDoc (Document Classification), Cross-lingual Intent Classification, POS Tagging, and Named Entity Recognition.

They were chosen based on the intersection of languages for which POS labels are available in the Universal Dependencies dataset and the languages supported by the mNMT model.
They use both automatic evaluation (perplexity and BLEU) and human evaluation using ACUTE-EVAL self-chat pairwise comparisons measuring engagingness, interestingness, and humanness.
By a significant gap.
They use a translation-based pipeline via the Google Translate API: target query → English query → English response → target response (used with the Poly-encoder).
Chinese, French, Indonesian, Italian, Korean, and Japanese.

For recurrent models the baseline is a standard bidirectional RNN model with attention, trained with Nematus, operating on the sentence level and not seeing additional context. For Transformer models the baseline is a standard context-agnostic Transformer (Transformer-base as in Vaswani et al., 2017).

Recurrent context-aware models: concat22, s-hier, s-t-hier, s-hier-to-2 (with weight tying variants). 
Transformer context-aware models: concat22, concat21, and the Voita et al. (2018) context-aware Transformer (separate context encoder with shared layers and gating).
English and German.

They use a simple EL algorithm that links each mention to the entity with the highest commonness score (computed from Wikipedia anchor links), use that commonness as the linking confidence g, and apply the Ganea and Hofmann (2017) heuristic within a document to resolve generic person mentions to more specific ones.

They use two layers of BiLSTMs to encode context, concatenate the context, mention string, KB type representation and EL score, feed this into a three-layer MLP to produce a mention representation, and score types by dot product with learned type embeddings.
FIGER (GOLD) and BBN
One.
Precision, recall, F1-score, support, and accuracy (including accuracy without the ‘O’-tag).
The model gives an F1-score of 0.89 for the concept recognition task.
Yes; the implementation with demo and dataset is available at https://github.com/jitinkrishnan/NASA-SE.

The BIO Labelling Scheme uses the following entity labels: abb (abbreviations such as TRL), grp (groups or individuals like Electrical Engineers or Project Manager), syscon (system concepts like engineering unit, product, hardware, software), opcon (operational concepts like decision analysis process, technology maturity assessment, system requirements review), seterm (generic SE terms like project, mission, key performance parameter, audit), event (event-like info such as Pre-Phase A, Phase A, Phase B), org (organizations such as NASA, aerospace industry), art (artifact or instrument names such as AS1300), cardinal (numerical values like 1, 100, one), loc (location-like entities such as component facilities or centralized facility), and mea (measures, features, or behaviors such as cost, risk, feasibility).
BERT
Roughly 3700 sentences.
Precision, recall and F-measure.
Position of sentence, sentence length and tense; content features, qualifying adjectives and meta-discourse features.

They used word embeddings learned by the classical word2vec algorithm, paragraph vectors (PV-DM), and BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE) inspired by Tang et al.’s Sentiment-Specific Word Embedding (SSWE).
No; they used existing datasets (ACL, MixedAbs) and the AZ-annotated Argumentative Zoning Corpus rather than creating their own annotations.

Sentence embeddings were learned from a large text corpus using three methods: (1) averaging the word2vec word vectors for the words in a sentence, (2) training paragraph vectors (PV-DM) which assign a unique vector to each paragraph, and (3) training BAS-specific word embeddings (BSWE) inspired by sentiment-specific embeddings and then averaging those word vectors for a sentence.
Rhetorical sentence classification, also known as argumentative zoning (AZ), is a process of assigning rhetorical status to the extracted sentences.

They crawled two credit-based freemium blackmarket sites (YouLikeHits and Like4Like) between February and April 2019, created dummy accounts to participate and recorded Tweet IDs of tweets posted for gaining retweets, then used Twitter’s REST API to collect the tweet objects and the authors' timelines.

They compare to state-of-the-art Twitter spam detection methods (Spam Detection 1: Wu et al.; Spam Detection 2: Rajdev et al.) and feature-concatenation classifiers trained with Random Forest, MLP, and SVM.
English
They mainly concentrate on freemium services: social-share services, credit-based services, and auto-time retweet services.
They use English and Chinese for the two-language setting and English, French, and Chinese for the three-language setting, initializing from a 15-language pre-trained XLM.

A Transformer-based autoregressive decoder in the sequence-to-sequence XNLG model that supports multiple languages, uses language tag embeddings to distinguish source and target languages, and generates the target sequence conditioned on the encoder representations.
The encoder is a Transformer-based bidirectional encoder (part of the sequence-to-sequence XNLG model) that supports multiple languages and uses language tag embeddings.
Their baseline is XLM, a state-of-the-art cross-lingual pre-trained Transformer (Lample and Conneau 2019).

They use an Amazon Mechanical Turk triple-pairing task where judges select the correct prompt-story pairings (prompt/story pairing accuracy), and a blind pairwise human preference test comparing hierarchical generation versus non-hierarchical generation.
Perplexity on the test set and prompt ranking accuracy.

They evaluate: (1) Language Models — gated convolutional language (GCNN) and GCNN with self-attention; (2) seq2seq — LSTMs and convolutional seq2seq (including Conv seq2seq with decoder self-attention); (3) Ensemble — an ensemble of two Conv seq2seq with self-attention models; and (4) KNN — TF‑IDF/FASTTEXT vectors with FAISS retrieval.
No; the paper does not use pre-trained embeddings like BERT.

170 annotators.

Neighborhood overlap measures: down, up, in, out, and bi (modified Jaccard-style overlaps between authors' and targets' follower/following/neighborhood sets). User-based features: counts of friends and followers, verified status, and number of tweets posted within six-month timeline snapshots.
Aggressive, Repetitive, Intentional, Visible, and Imbalanced.

The authors state that cyberbullying definitions contain a strict subset of the following criteria: aggression (AGGR), repetition (REP), harmful intent (HARM), visibility among peers (PEER), and power imbalance (POWER).

They evaluated similarity with the training data using Phrase Overlap and K‑gram‑Jaccard measures, assessed syntactic correctness using the Link Grammar Parser, and judged humor content subjectively via human evaluation.
The joke data came from CrowdTruth and Subreddits.

By 0.105 in MRR (about 25.1% relative improvement) and 6.1% absolute improvement in Hits@10.

Raw agreement: Phase 1 — scene .92, function .95, construal .90; Phase 2 — scene .93, function .90, construal .89. Cohen’s kappa: Phase 1 — scene .90, function .93, construal .88; Phase 2 — scene .92, function .88, construal .88.

They used the Chinese translation of The Little Prince, tokenized automatically with Jieba and manually corrected to follow Chinese PTB segmentation, manually identified adposition targets according to their criteria, ran StanfordNLP to get POS/dependency parses, and had three trained native Mandarin annotators label supersenses (25% cooperatively; the rest independently by the three annotators followed by cooperative adjudication), with annotations stored in CoNLL-U-Lex.
20,287 tokens.

They use the training dataset (with five features: ID, title, author, text, label) containing "20800 odd number of samples"; the provided test dataset is not used because it has no labels and test sets are selected randomly from the training data when evaluating models.

Numerical features; language model probabilities; word clusters (k‑means on embeddings); topic models (LDA topic distributions); part‑of‑speech tags (n‑gram bag‑of‑words); and the essay text represented as idf‑weighted bigram bag‑of‑words.

Gradient boosted trees trained with gradient-based one-side sampling as implemented in Microsoft’s LightGBM.

Further analysis and validation to determine whether LDA topic features reflect genuine modeling power or are an artifact of the dataset, specifically by validating with splits based on essay topics if the topics are released.
A tuned Logistic Regression with the same class weights for the class imbalance.

Sentence alignment quality is evaluated using a 5-point scale: 1 = Wrong alignment; 2 = Partial alignment with slightly compositional translational equivalence; 3 = Partial alignment with compositional translation and additional or missing information; 4 = Correct alignment with compositional translation and few additional or missing information; 5 = Correct alignment and fully compositional translation.

Manually by two annotators who rated audio-text alignment on a 3-point scale: 1 = wrong alignment; 2 = partial alignment (some words or sentences may be missing); 3 = correct alignment (allowing non-spoken syllables at start or end).
Yes — the selective gate is designed to handle one-sentence bags by dynamically assigning low gating values to wrongly labeled instances to prevent noisy representations from propagating.

By weighting each training sample inversely proportional to the estimated artifact-induced label probability P(y|h) (estimated via K-fold cross-prediction) — specifically using w = 1/((1−ε)P(y|h)+ε) and normalizing weights to keep class priors — the training distribution becomes artifact-balanced across labels, mitigating the bias pattern; the smoothing term ε improves robustness by preventing extreme weights when P(y|h) is near zero.
SNLI, MultiNLI, JOCI and SICK.
The entropy strategy.
3,685,984 unique tweets.
10K sentences and 250K tokens.

They explored a feedforward network with context window and a bidirectional LSTM (biLSTM), using character-level max-pooled CNNs for character embeddings, and prediction via either a softmax layer or a CRF layer.

A rule-based tagger designed by Rashel et al., which tags named entities and multi-word expressions using a dictionary, uses MorphInd to tag the remaining words, and applies 15 hand-crafted post-processing rules.

A sample of 10,417 users was drawn from a database of more than 400 million publicly available tweets posted by more than 100,000 users who announced their pregnancy; two professional annotators manually labeled users as “bot” (if they did not appear to be posting personal information), “non-bot,” or “unavailable” (if the Twitter site could not be viewed), with inter-annotator agreement κ = 0.93 on 1,000 overlapping annotations. Upon resolving disagreements, 413 (4%) users were annotated as “bot,” 7,849 (75.35%) as “non-bot,” and 2,069 (19.9%) as “unavailable.”

Use the existing system (Botometer) as a feature by taking its score and tuning a threshold (0.47 via 5‑fold cross validation), then train a gradient boosting classifier (optimized with 5‑fold CV, exponential loss, 200 estimators, learning rate 0.1) while using SMOTE to address class imbalance. Augment with health-focused features not in Botometer: tweet diversity, URL score, mean daily posts, topic heterogeneity via LDA, mean post length, profile-picture face detection, and person-name detection in usernames.

Tasks such as adverse drug reaction detection, syndromic surveillance, subject recruitment for cancer trials, characterizing drug abuse, population-level studies, and longitudinal user-level analyses (e.g., mining health information from cohorts of pregnant women).
Yes — they are from a customer support dataset sourced from Twitter.

They used a Light Gradient Boosting Machine (LGBM), an implementation of fast gradient boosting on decision trees (library by Microsoft).

The authors created PICONET by searching PubMed on April 11, 2019 for 363,078 structured abstracts (filters: Article Types=Clinical Trial, Species=Humans, Languages=English), selecting abstract section headings representative of Population/Intervention/Outcome via lemmatization and manual inspection (to avoid a keyword-based approach), keeping full sections (with multi-labeling for P and I and inclusion of negative examples), and cleaning/filtering the data (language identification, removal of missing values, unicode cleaning, and keeping sequences between 5 and 200 words).
Unanswerable

They suffered from ambiguous and redundant labels due to keyword-based labeling and overly general section headings (e.g., "methods"), and some prior datasets split multi-sentence P sections into multiple sentences, losing contextual richness and producing poor candidates for the corresponding labels.

They explore subject-verb agreement, noun-determiner agreement, noun-attributive-adjective agreement, and subject-predicate-adjective agreement.
Amazon, Yelp, IMDB, MR, MPQA, Subj, TREC
word-LSTM, word-CNN, and a fine-tuned BERT base-uncased classifier.

The baselines are "All Sarcasm" (assumes every instance is sarcastic) and "Random" (randomly assigns each instance as sarcastic or non-sarcastic).
Twitter and Amazon reviews.

The training data was 80% of datasets from two domains: Amazon reviews from the publicly available sarcasm corpus of Filatova (2012), and a Twitter dataset (tweets collected in Feb–Mar 2016 with exactly one trailing hashtag; #sarcasm labeled sarcastic and #happiness, #sadness, #anger, #surprise, #fear, #disgust labeled non-sarcastic).
The best model (LSTM and GRU with Attention) achieved Not Offensive P=0.92 R=0.99 F1=0.96; Offensive P=0.96 R=0.68 F1=0.77; weighted average P=0.93 R=0.88 F1=0.88; Macro-F1=0.89.

Classical models: two SVMs (linear and RBF), a linear classifier with Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGDC), Multinomial Naïve Bayes and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes. Deep learning models: Pooled GRU; Stacked LSTM with Attention; LSTM and GRU with Attention; 2D Convolution with Pooling; GRU with Capsule; LSTM with Capsule and Attention; and BERT.
LSTM and GRU with Attention
No; annotations were performed by a team of three volunteers using LightTag, with an author and two extra human judges resolving complete disagreements.

They train five classical machine learning models: two SVMs (linear and RBF), a linear classifier with Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGDC), Multinomial Naïve Bayes, and Bernoulli Naïve Bayes. They also experiment with six deep learning models: Pooled GRU, Stacked LSTM with Attention, LSTM and GRU with Attention, 2D Convolution with Pooling, GRU with Capsule, LSTM with Capsule and Attention, and BERT.

The published dataset contains 567 Catalan and 343 Basque reviews (910 reviews in total).
Three annotators.

The sentence construction component (GF Grammar Encoder) identifies appropriate GF rules to build a GF grammar for a sentence from its recognized structure and components, assigning an initial GF rule per structure (Table 2). It then selects sets of GF rules (following two conventions: resulting production types must be one of those in Table 2 and rules are chosen top-to-bottom from Table 3) based on known input/output types to create constructors and generate supporting operators for the GF grammar.
NLG for Annotated Ontologies; Intermediate Representation for Wiki Pages
Unanswerable
A stacked character-based LSTM with two layers, followed by a hidden layer and a softmax output; language embedding vectors are concatenated to the LSTM inputs and the hidden layer.

They perform hierarchical clustering on the learned language vectors from the full model (990 languages), and the resulting clusters (e.g., Germanic subset in Figure 5) closely match established genetic relationships.
Yes — they report testing MagiCoder in daily pharmacovigilance activities with preliminary qualitative results showing it drastically reduces the amount of work required for report revision.

Yes; they hypothesize that MagiCoder’s pure word-by-word syntactical recognition (exacerbated by stemming issues and uncontrolled synonym expansion) and the strict selection criterion requiring complete coverage make it robust on short, simple descriptions but lead to failures on longer/more complex descriptions (more tokens, morphological variations, connectives/negations and redundancy).

Definition of ad hoc data structures; Preprocessing of the original ADR description; Word-by-word linear scan of the description and voting task; Weight calculation (Criterion one: Coverage; Criterion two: Type of Coverage; Criterion three: Coverage Distance; Criterion four: Coverage Density); Selection, ordering and release of winning terms.

By building hash-table meta-dictionaries (DictByWord and DictByWordStem) that allow constant-time lookup of which LLTs contain a given word and then performing a single word-by-word linear scan of the narrative that "votes" terms.

Two CopyRNN encoder-decoder models with a copying mechanism were trained: CopySci (trained on KP20k scientific abstracts) and CopyNews (trained on KPTimes newspaper articles).

They are manually curated by editors who revise algorithm-proposed tags and provide additional tags used by a taxonomy team. This contrasts with existing datasets whose annotations come from readers (who tend to assign extractive keyphrases with few absent keyphrases) or authors (whose annotations are less constrained and include seldom-used variants or misspellings).

SGNN

The event prediction task is evaluated on the standard multiple choice narrative cloze (MCNC) dataset (Granroth-Wilding and Clark, 2016), following Li et al. (2018a), using the SGNN framework with their input event embeddings replaced by the intent- and sentiment-enhanced event embeddings.

The paper uses the hard similarity dataset (small: 230 event pairs from Weber et al. (2018) and a big dataset of 2,000 event pairs annotated by the authors), the transitive sentence similarity dataset (Kartsaklis and Sadrzadeh, 2014; 108 pairs), the standard MCNC script event prediction dataset (Granroth-Wilding and Clark, 2016), and the S&P 500 index stock market prediction dataset.
No; the paper does not compare to models like fastText.

Yes — the model performs better on morphologically complex words and is expected to perform even better for languages with richer morphology (e.g., Turkish, German) than for morphologically impoverished English.

Model word vectors are evaluated based on ranking the word pairs according to their cosine similarity, and then measuring the correlation (using Spearman’s ρ) between model judgments and human judgments.

They compared to Addition, Addition-proj, the Neural Reasoner (NR), and the Attentive Reader (AR).
A one-stage RNN system whose RNN structure is the same as the last stage (a 2-layer BLSTM) and is directly trained to recognize the dialect category.

They achieve a ~10% relative accuracy increase over the baseline, with the two-stage system reaching 88.88% overall (87.72% for ≤3s, 90.04% for >3s) versus baseline 78.85%. Some dialects reach very high accuracies (Shanghai 98.8%, Hefei 99.8%), and the two-stage system has slightly higher accuracy and faster training than the three-stage system.

Building Educational Applications
There is no clear winner: MODULAR is better at reducing bias and has higher accuracy, while CONCURRENT produces more fluent responses and preserves meaning better.
They were made by English Wikipedia editors as part of Wikipedia revisions (i.e., a Wiki community effort).

By framing neutralization as a translation task and using two algorithms: MODULAR — a BERT-based detector that tags subjective words feeding an LSTM editor with copy and coverage mechanisms and a join embedding that enriches encoder states for words to be changed — and CONCURRENT — a BERT encoder with an attentional LSTM decoder (with copy and coverage) trained end-to-end (pretrained as a denoising autoencoder and fine-tuned with a token-weighted loss) to generate a neutralized version of the input.
American Sign Language (ASL) recognition using both RGB video and skeletal (3D body joint) data.
70% (Max CNN-LSTM)
